coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence switch-on detection of alkaline phosphatase activity and adenosine triphosphate using vitamin B6 cofactor conjugated Mn-doped ZnS QDs
coredata.dc:creator: Dhanshri, Sonkeshriya
Sahoo, Suban K.
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a fluorescent nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS obtained through post-functionalization of l-cysteine capped Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots (l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs) with pyridoxal 5′-phosphate (PLP). The functionalization process involves the formation of a Schiff base between PLP and l-Cyst, leading to the quenching of fluorescence emission at 603 nm. The quenched PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS acts as a highly sensitive fluorescent turn-on probe for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). ALP catalytically dephosphorylates the functionalized PLP into pyridoxal (PL). In contrast, the interaction of ATP with the functionalized PLP suppresses the electron transfer process leading to the recovery of fluorescence emission at 603 nm. This nanoprobe exhibits exceptionally high sensitivity enabling the estimation of ALP and ATP concentrations as low as 0.006 U/L and 0.14 µM, respectively. The practical applicability of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS is demonstrated by accurately quantifying spiked ALP and ATP in human serum and plasma. This work highlights the potential of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS as a versatile and efficient nanoprobe for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mn-doped ZnS QDs
Fluorescent nanoprobe
Vitamin B6 cofactor
Alkaline phosphatase activity
Adenosine triphosphate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of l-Cyst capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs
Interaction of PLP with l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs
Procedure to detect ALP activity and ATP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence (λex = 310 nm) spectra of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs (inset showing an image of the QDs under UV lamp at 365 nm). (b) Comparative FT-IR spectra of l-Cyst, l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs, and PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs. (c) TEM image of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs. (d) The fluorescence selectivity study of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs with the addition of different vitamin B6 cofactors.
(a) Fluorescence spectral changes of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs upon the incremental addition of PLP. (b) The calibration curve for estimating the detection limit of PLP.

The bar graph representation of change in fluorescence intensity of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs (PLP@QDs) at 603 nm with ALP and various bioactive analytes in a competitive environment, highlighting the selectivity and specificity.
(a) Fluorescence spectral changes of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs upon the addition of increase amounts of ALP. (b) The calibration curve for estimating detection limit of ALP.

The bar graph representation of change in fluorescence intensity of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs (PLP@QDs) at 603 nm with ATP and various bioactive analytes in a competitive environment, highlighting the selectivity and specificity.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs varied with increasing amounts of ATP. (b) Calibration curve used to estimate ATP detection limit.
Schematic illustration for the interaction occurred between l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS and PLP, which employed for the detection of ALP activity and ATP.
Results of real sample analysis to quantify ALP activity with PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs.
Results of real sample analysis to quantify ATP with PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence switch-on detection of alkaline phosphatase activity and adenosine triphosphate using vitamin B6 cofactor conjugated Mn-doped ZnS QDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a fluorescent nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS obtained through post-functionalization of l-cysteine capped Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots (l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs) with pyridoxal 5′-phosphate (PLP). The functionalization process involves the formation of a Schiff base between PLP and l-Cyst, leading to the quenching of fluorescence emission at 603 nm. The quenched PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS acts as a highly sensitive fluorescent turn-on probe for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). ALP catalytically dephosphorylates the functionalized PLP into pyridoxal (PL). In contrast, the interaction of ATP with the functionalized PLP suppresses the electron transfer process leading to the recovery of fluorescence emission at 603 nm. This nanoprobe exhibits exceptionally high sensitivity enabling the estimation of ALP and ATP concentrations as low as 0.006 U/L and 0.14 µM, respectively. The practical applicability of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS is demonstrated by accurately quantifying spiked ALP and ATP in human serum and plasma. This work highlights the potential of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS as a versatile and efficient nanoprobe for biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a versatile enzyme known for its catalytic role in cleaving phosphate groups from various molecular substrates in biological processes. It exists in four distinct forms: intestinal alkaline phosphatase (IAP), placental alkaline phosphatase (PLAP), germ cell alkaline phosphatase (GCAP), and tissue nonspecific alkaline phosphatase (TNAP). While its primary functions involve nucleotide-to-nucleoside metabolism and transphosphorylation, ALP's importance is steadily growing in modern biosensing applications [1]. The abnormal ALP levels in serum is closely related to many diseases, like bone disease [2], liver dysfunction, diabetes [3,4], heart failure, ovarian and breast cancer [5], as well as osteoblastic bone cancer and liver tumors [6,7]. ALP biosensors can also act as important biomarkers for obstructive jaundice, Paget's disease, heart failure, and diabetes [8]. In adults, the normal range of ALP is usually between 40 and 190 U/L [9], while children and pregnant women tend to have higher levels. Several assays have been proposed for the detection of ALP, such as colorimetry [10-15], electrochemical [16-18], and fluorescent assays [19]. Among all, fluorometric assays have been gaining great attention due to their rapidity, simplicity and high sensitivity [19,20]. Fluorescent strategies for ALP detection can be categorized into switch-off [21,22], switch-on, [23-27] and ratiometric, with “switch-on” method being particularly popular.

Similar to ALP, the detection of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is also equally important because of its significant role in the biochemical process. The structural composition of ATP has a negatively charged phosphate group, aromatic adenosine, and ribose with polyhydroxy. ATP functions as a universal energy source for many enzymatic reactions. It is known as the “energy currency” and an “important metabolite” [28]. ATP plays an essential role in a range of metabolic processes both inside and outside of cells as an intracellular energy source and an extracellular signaling mediator among the various phosphate anions [29,30]. The abnormal intracellular level of ATP can affect the immunity and growth of tumor cells, also cause several diseases like Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, Stargardt disease, numerous malignant tumors, pancreatic, cardiac, vascular disease, hypoxia and poglycemia [31]. From the literature, various detection methods have been proposed for ATP detection, such as colorimetry, fluorometry, nuclear magnetic resonance, high-performance liquid chromatography, electrochemiluminescence, chemiluminescence and capillary electrophoresis [36-38]. In recent years, significant attention have been made to develop fluorescence-based detection methods for ATP because of the advantageous features of real-time reaction monitoring, high selectivity and sensitivity [32-43].

The nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (QDs), fluorescent polymer nanoparticles, fluorescent noble metal nanoclusters, and organic molecular probes are widely employed for designing fluorescence-based probes. Due to the broad and continuous excitation and emission spectra, excellent optical properties, outstanding photochemical stabilities, large Stokes shift, and long luminescence property, ALP and ATP fluorescent probes made of QDs have attracted significant interest in the biomedical field [30,44-46]. In recent years, to avoid the use of heavy-metals like cadmium, lead and mercury in the QDs core and also organic solvents in the synthesis process, Mn-doped ZnS QDs have received significant attention in the field of sensing due to their facile synthesis, low toxicity, low cost, high stability and excellent photoluminescence properties [47-50]. Considering the above facts in view, herein, we synthesized l-cysteine capped Mn-doped ZnS (l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS) QDs and studied its interaction with active vitamin B6 cofactor pyridoxal 5′-phosphate (PLP). The conjugation of PLP over the surface of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs quenched the fluorescence emission. ALP catalyzes the hydrolysis of PLP into pyridoxal (PL), which facilitate the albumin-bound PLP to cross the cell membranes [51]. Mimicking this biological process, the nano-assembly PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was employed further as a nanoprobe for the fluorescent turn-on detection of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity (Scheme 1 ). Interestingly, the PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs also showed a fluorescence switch-on response for adenosine triphosphate (ATP).

Analytical grade chemicals such as l-cysteine (l-Cyst), pyridoxal (PL), pyridoxamine (PYOA), PLP, pyridoxine (PY), zinc acetate (Zn(Ac)2·2H2O), manganese chloride (MnCl2), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O), ethanol (EtOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), Tris-HCl, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), ferric chloride (FeCl3), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), TBA dihydrogen phosphate (H2PO4 −), TBA fluoride (F−), TBA chloride (Cl−), TBA bromide (Br−), TBA acetate (CH3COO–), TBA ammonium nitrate (NO3 –), TBA sulphate (SO4 2−), TBA dihydrogen phosphate (H2PO4 −), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcyst), proline (Pro), and methionine (Meth) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich.

The fluorescence spectra were measured from 300 to 800 nm by using Cary Eclipse Fluorescent Spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission slits were adjusted at 10 nm and 20 nm, respectively. To record the FT-IR spectra, a Shimadzu IR Affinity1 FTIR instrument was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of l-Cyst capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: A colloidal solution of l-cysteine capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs was obtained over a period of 45 min of continuous stirring with a magnetic stirrer. This was done by mixing 20 mL of 0.01 M l-cysteine, 10 mL of 0.1 M zinc acetate, and 1 mL of 0.5 M manganese chloride. After that, 10 mL of 0.1 M sodium sulphide was added to the mixture dropwise, followed by stirred vigorously for 20 min. The colloidal solution was aged for two hours at 50 °C and kept at room temperature for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of PLP with l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: All solutions were prepared with Millipore water. For all sensing experiments, 20 µL of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs from stock was diluted with 1980 µL of water to prepare the working solution of probe. Vitamin B6 cofactors PL, PLP, PY, and PYOA (10 µL, 1 mM) were added to the probe solution. PLP induced fluorescence quenching, which was then used to conduct a fluorescence titration investigation. The cofactor was added in small amounts between 0 and 8.9 µM to observe the quenching effect. To evaluate the effects of various PLP concentrations on the probe, fluorescence spectra were gathered and used to create a calibration curve. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the equation, LOD = 3α/slope of the calibration curve, where α represents the relative standard deviation (RSD) of ten blank samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure to detect ALP activity and ATP
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mg of ALP was dissolved in 1 mLof 0.1 M Tris-HCl (pH = 8.0) to create the ALP stock solution. The stock solution was further diluted with buffer solution to obtain the working concentration of ALP of 0.01 mg/mL. The reported procedure was followed for detecting ALP activity [51]. For detecting ALP in real samples, the human serum and plasma sample collected from a healthy volunteer was diluted with water ten times and spiked with known amount of ALP. The spiked ALP samples were added to PLP@L-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The calculated ALP activity was compared with the amount of ALP added to estimate the recovery percentage.

Stock solutions of 1 mM ATP and other analytes were prepared in Millipore water. The selectivity study of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was performed with the different analytes (25 µL, 1 mM) such as Na+, K+, Cl−, F−, SO4 2−, CH3COO–, NO3 –, Mg2+, ADP, AMP, Pro and Meth. The PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs showed a selective fluorescence enhancement with ATP, which restored the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence sensitivity switch-on phenomenon was confirmed by performing the titration of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs with ATP. For specificity experiments, the fluorescence spectra of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs were recorded in the presence of ATP (25 µL, 1 mM) and co-presence of equimolar amount of other interfering analytes. To assess the practical utility of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs as a fluorescence restoration for ATP, the concentration of ATP was determined in real human serum and plasma samples. The samples were diluted ten times with water, and a known amount of ATP was added to the samples. The spiked sample was added to PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs and the recovery percentage was determined and compared [32].

The l-Cyst capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs were synthesized by the reported procedure [52]. The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra supported the formation of Mn-doped ZnS QDs (Fig. 1
a). The fluorescence emission at 603 nm, whereas a sloppy and broad absorption spectrum with a peak at 296 nm was observed for the as-synthesized QDs. FT-IR was used to investigate the properties of the QDs and enabled a deeper understanding of the characteristics. The analysis revealed the presence of –OH, –CH, and -SH functional groups in pure l-Cyst at 3018, 2905, and 2577 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1 b). The –SH peak of l-Cyst was disappeared upon the formation of l-Cyst capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs. The number of peaks in the fingerprint region of pure l-Cyst are also declined in l-Cyst capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs. Further, the statistical analysis of three TEM images was conducted to calculate the diameter of Mn-ZnS quantum dots (QDs), with an average value of 3.1 nm (Fig. 1 c). The measured X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs confirmed the presence of O 1s, C 1s, N 1s, Zn 2p, S 2p and Mn 2p ( Fig. S1 ). The deconvulated spectra of Zn 2p showed peaks at 1021.5 eV and 1044.8 eV representing2p(3∕2)and 2p(1∕2), respectively. The binding energy the peaks supported the presence of Zn-S bonding [53]. The deconvulated weak peaks of Mn2p(3∕2)and Mn 2p(1∕2) at 641.2 eV and 653.5 eV respectively confirmed the presence of Mn in ZnS.

The vitamin B6 cofactors such as pyridoxal, pyridoxine, pyridoxamine, and PLP are essential for a range of physiological and metabolic processes, including the creation of energy, the development of red blood cells, and chemical communication within the nervous system. Amongst these, PLP is the most active B6 vitamer and works as a cofactor for many proteins and enzymes [54]. When the l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was interacted with the four vitamers, the fluorescence emission at 603 nm of QDs was selectively quenched in the presence of PLP (Fig. 1 d). The –NH2 group of functionalized l-Cyst is expected to form imine linkage with the –CHO group of PLP. Analysis by FT-IR supported the appearance of the characteristic peak for the imine linkage at 1560 cm−1 ( Fig. 1 b). The decoration of PLP over the surface of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs via forming imine linkage allowed the electron transfer from the excited QDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PLP that quenched the fluorescence emission at 603 nm [51].

The fluorescence titration of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs with the successive incremental addition of PLP resulted in a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 603 nm (Fig. 2
a). The decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 603 nm showed good linearity for PLP from 2 µM to 6.9 µM (Fig. 2 b). From the calibration curve, the LOD for PLP was estimated as 1.18 µM. Compared to the available analytical methods that required potentially dangerous substances like cyanide, bisulfate, or chlorite for the detection of PLP [54], the use of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs as PLP nanoprobe is analytically simple, sensitive and rapid.

The ALP enzyme catalytically dephosphorylated the PLP-bound albumin to PL-bound albumin in the biological process. The fluorescence emission of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was quenched by PLP but not in the presence of PL, which provides an ideal platform to mimic the biological process for the development of a switch-on nanoprobe to detect ALP activity. The nanoprobe, i.e., PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was prepared by mixing 30 µL (1 mM) of PLP with the l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs. The nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was interacted with ALP (18 µL, 0.086 U/L) and other bioactive analytes (18 µL, 1 mM). The fluorescence spectra of nanoprobe were recorded after incubating at 37 °C for 30 min (Fig. 3 and Fig. S2 a). The quenched emission spectra of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was restored selectively by ALP, which indicates that the QDs can be developed as a switch-on probe for ALP. The regaining of fluorescence emission of QDs was occurred because of the ALP-directed catalytic conversion of PLP-bound QDs into PL-bound QDs. The fluorescence switch-on of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs by ALP (18 µL, 0.086 U/L) was also examined in the co-presence of other interfering bioactive analyte (18 μL, 1 × 10−3 M) to examine the specificity. As shown in Fig. 3 (Fig. S2 b), no noticeable changes observed with tested interfering analytes that supported the developed nanoprobe can be utilized as ALP sensor.

The sensitivity of the nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs towards ALP was examined by performing a fluorescence titration experiment. To estimate the sensitivity, increasing amounts of ALP were added to separate vials containing a 2 mL solution of nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs (Tris-HCl, pH = 8.0), and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the fluorescent intensity at 603 nm of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was enhanced gradually with the increase in the amounts of ALP. The calibration plot showed good linearity from 0.01 U/L to 0.086 U/L (Fig. 4 b), with an estimated detection limit of 0.006 U/L. The lower detection limit supported the ability of the nanoprobe to detect and track ALP levels in biological samples selectively and sensitively.[19] Also, the developed nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was analytically comparable with the reported fluorescent ALP probes ( Table S1 ). The practical utility of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was validated by quantifying ALP concentration in real biological samples, i.e., human serum and plasma. As summarized in Table 1 , the satisfactory recovery percentage supported the ability of nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs to monitor ALP levels in biological samples.

The PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was also employed as fluorescence switch-on probe to detect ATP. The fluorescence spectra of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs were recorded by adding analytes (25 µL, 1 mM), such as ATP, ADP, AMP, Na+, K+, Cl−, F−, SO4 2−, Br−, CH3COO–, NO3 –, Mg2+, Pro and Meth. The PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence at 603 nm was enhanced selectively and instantaneously in the presence of ATP (Fig. 5 and Fig. S3 a). Other examined analytes failed to disturb the fluorescence profile of QDs. The possible binding interaction of ATP molecules occurs with the functionalized PLP through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding [35]. The interaction of ATP over the surface of QDs suppressed the energy transfer process from excited QDs to the PLP, which restored the fluorescence emission at 603 nm. The competitive experiments were also performed to examine the specificity, where the PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence spectra were recorded in the presence of ATP (25 µL, 1 mM) and co-presence of equimolar amount of interfering analytes (Fig. 5 and Fig. S3 b). The competitive experiments revealed no obvious interference by the examined interfering analytes for the detection of ATP by PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs.

In summary, the developed l-cysteine capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs was post-functionalized with vitamin B6 cofactor PLP. The functionalization of PLP by forming imine linkage with functionalized l-Cyst quenched the fluorescence emission of l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs. The PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs was developed as a fluorescent switch-on probe for the selective detection of ALP and ATP. Under optimized conditions of pH, incubation time of 30 min at 37 °C, PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs showed fluorescence turn-on response for ALP. The addition of ALP catalytically converted the PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs to PL@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs. The nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs also showed instantaneous fluorescence response towards ATP. The interaction of ATP over the surface of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs via non-covalent interaction suppressed the electron transfer process that switch-on the fluorescence emission at 603 nm. With the nanoprobe PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS QDs, the concentration of ALP and ATP can be detected down to 0.006 U/L and 0.14 µM, respectively. The practical utility of PLP@l-Cyst-Mn-ZnS was successfully validated by quantifying ALP and ATP in human serum and plasma samples.

Sonkeshriya Dhanshri: Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Suban K. Sahoo: Conceptualization, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.111288.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alkaline phosphatase: a reliable endogenous partner for drug delivery and diagnostics

Farber, Cationized dextran nanoparticle-encapsulated CXCR4-siRNA enhanced correlation between CXCR4 expression and serum alkaline phosphatase in a mouse model of colorectal cancer, Int. J. Nanomed. (2012) 4159-4168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase by switching on gold nanoclusters fluorescence quenched by pyridoxal phosphate

Interfaces 6 (2014) 1979-1985.

Bioelectron. 85 (2016) 220-225.
